title: Add SKFilteredImage and related
body:
**Description of Change**

This is an mage view that has a filter/rendering pipeline that can allow for more exciting images:

```xaml
<controls:SKFilteredImage HeightRequest="200">
    <media:SKFilterPipeline Source="{Binding Image}">
        <media:SKInvertFilter />
        <media:SKBlurFilter SigmaX="6" SigmaY="6" />
        <media:SKSepiaFilter />
        <media:SKHighContrastFilter Factor="0.5" />
    </media:SKFilterPipeline>
</controls:SKFilteredImage>
```

Even though this is right now a new view, I also want to make this into an ImageSource for a one-time render for any image view control or ImageSource property.

| Original | Filtered |
| :--------: | :-------: |
| ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1096616/93465736-7ad4f080-f8eb-11ea-96d7-7bd87e66a7a3.png) | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1096616/93466245-3dbd2e00-f8ec-11ea-9194-de8bc699c55f.png) |

**Bugs Fixed**

- Related to issue #96

**API Changes**

<!-- REPLACE THIS COMMENT

 - `object Cell.OldPropertyName => object Cell.NewPropertyName`

comments.author: mattleibow
comments.body:
Closing this draft after letting it rest for ~5 years. 🙂

The idea here is genuinely interesting — declarative, XAML-composable image effects is a compelling pattern on paper — but after thinking it through carefully, the concept doesn't hold up strongly enough against how real apps actually work:

- The most common visual treatments (blur-behind, frosted glass, loading placeholders) all require **compositor-level** effects, not per-image SkiaSharp paint pipelines. Those are architecturally different problems.
- Cases like grayscale for disabled/out-of-stock states are real, but the right answer is likely a focused, single-purpose attached property — not a full filter pipeline.
- For apps that need image filters at scale, server-side image transformation (Cloudinary, imgix, etc.) or platform-native approaches are a better fit.

The surviving real use case — something like a `Grayscale` property on an image view for state-driven UX — is worth revisiting, but as a much smaller, focused feature rather than a general filter pipeline.

Closing in favour of potentially revisiting a narrower version of this if the need comes up concretely from users. The `dev/filters` branch will stay around if we ever want to reference the approach.
